perturbation.original_text: 11.1 The Company's holiday year runs from 1 January to 31 December (the Holiday Year). The Executive is entitled to 25 days' paid holiday in addition to the usual public or bank holidays in England) in every Holiday Year, to be taken at times convenient to the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: 11.1 The Company's holiday year runs from 1 January to 31 December (the Holiday Year). The Executive is entitled to 25 days' paid holiday, inclusive of all public and bank holidays, at times convenient to the Company.
perturbation.explanation: This change modifies the entitlement to holidays. It now includes public and bank holidays within the 25 days, potentially reducing the executive's actual time off. This contradicts statutory holiday entitlement requirements.
perturbation.law_citation: Regulation 13 of the Working Time Regulations 1998
perturbation.law_explanation: Regulation 13 of the Working Time Regulations 1998 mandates a minimum of 5.6 weeks' paid holiday, which can include bank holidays, but employers must clearly state that bank holidays are included. If the contract is ambiguous, the employee can argue they are entitled to both the stated holiday days and bank holidays, thus potentially violating employee rights under UK law.

perturbation.justification: The change in text introduces a potential contradiction to the Working Time Regulations 1998. The original text grants 25 days' paid holiday 'in addition to' bank holidays. The modified text includes bank holidays within the 25 days, potentially reducing the executive's overall holiday entitlement. The LLM-generated Law Explanation suggests that while bank holidays can be included, the contract must clearly state their inclusion, and ambiguity could lead to the employee claiming both. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the Working Time Regulations 1998. This regulation mandates a minimum holiday entitlement, and reducing the holiday allowance by including bank holidays within the existing allowance directly opposes this regulation.

perturbation.changed_text: 13.1 In this clause 13, Confidential Information means information (whether or not recorded in documentary form, or stored on any magnetic or optical disk or memory) relating solely to the business's core trade secrets, technical data that provides a direct competitive advantage, and specifically designated documents marked 'Confidential Trade Secret.' It explicitly excludes general business strategies, pricing information, employee data unrelated to trade secrets, and any information already known within the industry or publicly accessible through legal means. This definition applies only if the information is clearly marked and stored with high-level security measures.
perturbation.explanation: This significantly narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information,' removing many standard inclusions. It contradicts the broader legal understanding of what constitutes confidential information and trade secrets, making enforcement more difficult.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018 define trade secrets more broadly than the modified contract. By narrowly defining 'Confidential Information,' the contract fails to protect information that could be legally considered a trade secret, undermining the purpose of trade secret law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' compared to the original contract. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state that this narrower definition fails to protect information that could legally be considered a trade secret, undermining trade secret law. The original text included a broad range of information within the definition of "Confidential Information," while the changed text limits it to core trade secrets, technical data providing a direct competitive advantage, and specifically marked documents. This contradicts the broader legal understanding implied by the scraped snippet from The Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018, which suggests a more comprehensive approach to defining and protecting trade secrets. Though the snippet is incomplete, it refers to the Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018, which the LLM used to generate its law explanation; thus, the edited contract's definition does not align with legal standards.

perturbation.original_text: 15.2 The Company may in its sole and absolute discretion (whether or not any notice of termination has been given under sub clause 15.1) terminate this Agreement at any time and with immediate effect by giving notice in writing to the Executive that the Company is exercising its rights pursuant to this clause 15. If the Company elects to terminate the Executive's employment in this way, it will make, within 30 days, either the first instalment (of equal monthly instalments) of a, or an entire, payment in lieu of notice (Payment in Lieu) equal to the basic salary, benefit allowance and any benefits, as at the Termination Date, which the Executive would have been entitled to receive under this Agreement during the notice period referred to at sub clause 15.1 (or, if notice has already been given, during the remainder of the notice period), less all relevant deductions for income tax and National Insurance contributions. For the avoidance of doubt, the Payment in Lieu shall not include any element in relation to: (a) any bonus or discretionary payment(s) that might otherwise have been due during the period for which the Payment in Lieu is made; and (b) any payment in respect of any holiday entitlement that would have accrued during the period for which the Payment in Lieu is made.
perturbation.changed_text: 15.2 The Company may, at its sole discretion, terminate this Agreement at any time with immediate effect. In such case, the Company is not obligated to provide any payment in lieu of notice, severance, or any other form of compensation beyond the executive's accrued salary up to the termination date.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the obligation to provide Payment in Lieu (PILON), even though a 12-month notice period is stipulated elsewhere. Under UK law, removing the PILON without proper cause could be construed as unfair dismissal, particularly given the executive's senior role.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 86 of the Employment Rights Act 1996 implies a minimum notice period dependent on the length of service. Removing the entitlement to Payment in Lieu without valid reason or compensation for the required notice period could violate the employee's statutory rights and expose the company to claims of unfair dismissal.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Dismissal: your rights: Overview - GOV.UK Cookies on GOV.UK We use some essential cookies to make this website work. We’d like to set additional cookies to understand how you use GOV.UK, remember your settings and improve government services. We also use cookies set by other sites to help us deliver content from their services. You have accepted additional cookies. You can change your cookie settings at any time. You have rejected additional cookies. You can change your cookie settings at any time. Accept additional cookies Reject additional cookies View cookies Hide this message Skip to main content Dismissal: your rights Skip contents Overview Dismissal is when your employer ends your employment - they do not always have to give you notice. If you’re dismissed, your employer must show they’ve: a valid reason that they can justify acted reasonably in the circumstances They must also: be consistent - for example, not dismiss you for doing something that they let other employees do have investigated the situation fully before dismissing you - for example, if a complaint was made about you If you’re a part-time or fixed-term worker, you cannot be treated less favourably than a full-time or permanent employee. Notice period You must be given at least the notice stated in your contract or the statutory minimum notice period, whichever is longer. There are some situations where you can be dismissed immediately - for example, for violence. Getting your dismissal in writing You have the right to ask for a written statement from your employer giving the reasons why you’ve been dismissed if you’re an employee and have completed 2 years’ service (1 year if you started before 6 April 2012). Your employer must supply the statement within 14 days of you asking for it. Your employer must give you a written statement if you’re dismissed while you are on Statutory Maternity Leave . You get this: even if you’ve not asked for one regardless of how long you’ve worked for your employer Speak to your employer or check your employment status if you’re unsure of your employment status. perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the obligation to provide Payment in Lieu (PILON), which is contradictory to Section 86 of the Employment Rights Act 1996 that implies a minimum notice period, and the employee's right to receive statutory notice as stated in the scraped law snippet 2: 'You must be given at least the notice stated in your contract or the statutory minimum notice period, whichever is longer.' By removing the PILON, the contract effectively violates the employee's right to compensation during that statutory notice period, especially if the dismissal is without a valid justification.
